saida_bruta:
“You look familiar,” he said politely. “Weren’t you with the First Division during the war?”

“Why yes. I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I knew I’d seen you somewhere before.”

We spoke briefly about some wet, grey little villages in France. He apparently lived nearby, since he mentioned he had just bought a hydroplane and planned to try it out the next morning.

“Want to come with me, old sport? Just along the shore of the Sound.”

“What time?”

“Whenever suits you.”

I was about to ask his name when Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Having a good time now?” she asked.

“Much better.” I turned back to my new acquaintance. “This is a strange party for me. I haven’t even met the host. I live over there—” I gestured toward the distant, unseen hedge, “and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur with an invitation.”

For a moment he looked at me as though he didn’t understand.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“What!” I exclaimed. “Oh, forgive me.”

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m afraid I’m not much of a host.”

We talked briefly about a few damp, grey little villages in France. He evidently lived close by, since he mentioned he'd just bought a hydroplane and intended to try it out the next morning.

"Want to come along, old sport? Just along the shore of the Sound."

"Whenever it suits you best."

I was on the point of asking his name when Jordan looked over and smiled.

"Enjoying yourself now?" she asked.

"Much more so." I turned back to my new acquaintance. "This is an unusual party for me. I haven't even met the host yet. I live over there—" I waved toward the distant, invisible hedge, "and this man Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation."

For a moment he gazed at me as if he didn't quite follow.

"I'm Gatsby," he said abruptly.

"What!" I cried. "Oh, please forgive me."

"I thought you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm rather a poor host.

"What time?"

"Whatever suits you."

I was about to ask his name when Jordan looked over and smiled.

"Enjoying yourself now?" she asked.

"Much more so." I turned back to my new acquaintance. "This is an odd party for me. I haven't even met our host. I live just over there—" I gestured toward the distant, unseen hedge, "and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur with an invitation."

"I thought you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not much of a host."

saida_bruta:
"I've noticed you around before," he remarked politely. "Weren't you part of the First Division in the war?"

"That's right. I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry."

"I served with the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I figured we must have met somewhere."

We chatted for a bit about a handful of grim, gray villages in France. He apparently lived close by, since he mentioned he'd just purchased a seaplane and intended to try it out the following morning.

"Want to come along, old sport? Just a short trip along the Sound's edge."

"What time works?"

"Whenever you like."

I was on the verge of asking his name when Jordan glanced my way, smiling.

"Having fun now?" she asked.

"Much better." I turned to my new companion. "This is a strange party for me. I haven't even met the host. I live right over there—" I pointed toward the unseen hedge in the distance, "and this Gatsby fellow sent his driver with an invitation."

He looked at me for a moment as though he hadn't quite understood.

"I'm Gatsby," he said suddenly.

"What!" I exclaimed. "Oh, please forgive me."

"I assumed you knew, old sport. I'm not much of a host, I'm afraid."

"I've seen you somewhere before," he said politely. "Weren't you in the First Division during the war?"

"That's right. I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry."

"I was in the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I knew I'd seen you somewhere before."

We talked briefly about a few damp, gray villages in France. He apparently lived nearby, and mentioned he'd just bought a hydroplane, planning to try it out that morning.

"Want to come with me, old sport? Just a short ride along the shore of the Sound."

"What time?"

"Whenever suits you."

I was about to ask his name when Jordan looked over at me, smiling.

"Having a better time now?" she asked.

"Much better." I turned back to my new acquaintance. "This is an odd party for me. I haven't even met the host. I live right over there—" I gestured toward the hedge, far in the distance, "and this Gatsby fellow sent his chauffeur with an invitation."

For a moment he looked at me as though he hadn't quite understood.

"I'm Gatsby," he said suddenly.

"What!" I exclaimed. "Oh, please forgive me."

"I assumed you already knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not much of a host."
